choices.prompt: According to the module, why is 'the job is running' an insufficient signal for judging a streaming pipeline's health?
choices: A job can be running while its backlog grows every minute and results go stale, so degradation appears without any error
A running job always indicates that checkpoints have been corrupted and must be rebuilt
The running status only updates once per day, so it is always out of date by the time you read it
A running job consumes no resources, so utilization metrics cannot be collected while it runs
correct_answers: A job can be running while its backlog grows every minute and results go stale, so degradation appears without any error

choices.prompt: A Stream Analytics job shows input backlog climbing steadily while SU utilization sits at about 35%. Per the module's diagnostic rule, what is the correct interpretation and response?
choices: Low utilization with rising backlog points to a parallelism or skew problem — investigate the source partition ceiling or hot key rather than adding streaming units
High and low signals like this always mean the checkpoint was deleted, so restore the checkpoint before anything else
It is a clear capacity shortage, so the immediate fix is to raise streaming units until utilization reaches 100%
Backlog and utilization are unrelated, so the rising backlog should simply be ignored until users complain
correct_answers: Low utilization with rising backlog points to a parallelism or skew problem — investigate the source partition ceiling or hot key rather than adding streaming units

choices.prompt: An event hub has 8 partitions and a single consumer group. According to the module's rule tying processing parallelism to source parallelism, what happens if you throw 32 Spark cores at it?
choices: No more than 8 readers can pull concurrently, so the extra cores sit idle while 8 do the work
All 32 cores immediately read in parallel, quadrupling throughput past the partition ceiling
The partition count automatically increases to 32 to match the available cores
The job fails to start because core count must exactly equal partition count
correct_answers: No more than 8 readers can pull concurrently, so the extra cores sit idle while 8 do the work

choices.prompt: A Spark streaming job recovers cleanly from a five-minute node failure but loses data after a four-hour outage, even though the checkpoint was intact the whole time. Which setting most likely caused the loss?
choices: Source retention shorter than the outage — the unread events expired before the job came back
An undersized streaming-unit allocation that throttled the recovery read
A corrupted watermark that reset event time to wall-clock on restart
Too many partitions, which fragmented the checkpoint across nodes
correct_answers: Source retention shorter than the outage — the unread events expired before the job came back

choices.prompt: Which of the following are health signals the module names as the ones that actually matter for a streaming pipeline? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Input backlog — how much unprocessed data is sitting in the source
Lag — how far behind real time the output is
Resource utilization — such as SU utilization percentage or executor CPU/memory
The number of times the deployment YAML file has been edited this week
correct_answers: Input backlog — how much unprocessed data is sitting in the source
Lag — how far behind real time the output is
Resource utilization — such as SU utilization percentage or executor CPU/memory

choices.prompt: During the festival walkthrough, the metrics show SU utilization pinned near 100% while input backlog climbs steadily. Per the module, what does this pair of signals indicate and what is the correct response?
choices: A capacity-bound signal — arrival exceeds processing rate near a ceiling — so raise processing parallelism (more streaming units), provided the job is parallelizable and the source can feed it
A skew problem caused by one hot key, so the fix is a better partition key rather than more streaming units
A source partition ceiling, so the only valid response is to add more Event Hubs partitions
A retention shortfall, so retention must be extended before the backlog can drain
correct_answers: A capacity-bound signal — arrival exceeds processing rate near a ceiling — so raise processing parallelism (more streaming units), provided the job is parallelizable and the source can feed it

choices.prompt: The module describes scaling as having two independent dimensions. Which statements correctly identify what governs each dimension? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Ingestion capacity is governed by the source — Event Hubs partition count caps read parallelism and provisioned throughput caps ingress/egress rate
Processing capacity is the engine — streaming units in Stream Analytics, or executor count and cores in Spark
Scaling one stage without the others just moves the bottleneck rather than removing it
Ingestion capacity is governed by executor CPU and memory, while processing capacity is governed by partition count
correct_answers: Ingestion capacity is governed by the source — Event Hubs partition count caps read parallelism and provisioned throughput caps ingress/egress rate
Processing capacity is the engine — streaming units in Stream Analytics, or executor count and cores in Spark
Scaling one stage without the others just moves the bottleneck rather than removing it

choices.prompt: According to the module, why is over-provisioning singled out as a real cost concern specifically for streaming jobs?
choices: A streaming job runs continuously, twenty-four hours a day, so idle headroom is paid for every hour
A streaming job recomputes all historical results on every restart, multiplying the cost of spare capacity
Over-provisioning forces the source to retain events longer, which is the dominant cost
Idle streaming units automatically convert into additional partitions, inflating the source bill
correct_answers: A streaming job runs continuously, twenty-four hours a day, so idle headroom is paid for every hour

choices.prompt: The module says partition skew — one hot partition such as a single extremely chatty ferry — defeats scaling. Why does adding more resources fail to help, and what is the prescribed fix?
choices: One reader is overwhelmed while the rest idle, so more cores do not help; the fix is a better partition key, not more compute
The hot partition saturates the checkpoint, so the fix is to delete and rebuild the checkpoint
Skew exhausts source retention, so the fix is to extend retention to cover the hot key
Extra cores deadlock waiting on the hot reader, so the fix is to reduce the streaming-unit count
correct_answers: One reader is overwhelmed while the rest idle, so more cores do not help; the fix is a better partition key, not more compute

choices.prompt: Which statements reflect the operational discipline the module prescribes for clean recovery from interruptions? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Recovery rests on a replayable source — Event Hubs retains events so a restarted consumer resumes from its offset
A durable checkpoint lets Spark resume processing and window state exactly where it left off
Checkpoints must never be shared or deleted
Checkpoints should be deleted and rebuilt on each deployment to guarantee a clean restart
correct_answers: Recovery rests on a replayable source — Event Hubs retains events so a restarted consumer resumes from its offset
A durable checkpoint lets Spark resume processing and window state exactly where it left off
Checkpoints must never be shared or deleted

llm.prompt: Explain how reading backlog and resource utilization together lets you distinguish a capacity problem from a parallelism/skew problem, and what the correct scaling response is in each case.
llm.reference_answer: The two signals must be read together because each alone is ambiguous. Rising backlog combined with high utilization (for example SU utilization pinned near 100%) means arrival exceeds processing rate and you are near a ceiling, so the fix is more processing parallelism — raise streaming units in Stream Analytics or add executor cores in Spark. Rising backlog combined with low utilization instead points to a parallelism or skew problem: readers are limited by the source partition count, or one hot key dominates one reader while others idle. In that case adding streaming units or cores will not help; the right move is more partitions or a better partition key. Reading the pair is what tells you which lever to pull.

llm.prompt: Describe the rule connecting processing parallelism to source parallelism, why partition skew defeats scaling, and how each is correctly addressed.
llm.reference_answer: The rule is that processing parallelism cannot exceed source parallelism. Event Hubs partition count caps read parallelism — if a hub has 8 partitions, no more than 8 readers can pull concurrently per consumer group, so adding 32 Spark cores leaves the extra cores idle while 8 do the work. Conversely, adding partitions does nothing if the processing tier is already starved of compute, so the two dimensions must be scaled in proportion. Skew is a separate failure: one hot partition — a single extremely chatty producer like one ferry — overwhelms one reader while the rest idle, so the job will not scale no matter how many resources you add. The fix for skew is a better partition key so keys line up end to end, not more cores.

llm.prompt: Explain how a long-running streaming job recovers from interruptions, the role retention plays as a recovery-time budget, and why over-provisioning is a distinct cost concern for streaming.
llm.reference_answer: Recovery rests on two properties built in earlier modules: a replayable source and a durable checkpoint. Event Hubs retains events so a restarted consumer resumes from its committed offset, and a Spark checkpoint lets the job resume processing and window state exactly where it left off — checkpoints must never be shared or deleted. Retention is a recovery-time budget, not a storage afterthought: it must be long enough to cover the worst restart window from deployments and outages, because if a job is down longer than retention the unread events expire and are lost permanently. Over-provisioning is a distinct cost concern because a streaming job runs continuously, twenty-four hours a day, so idle headroom is paid for every hour. Right-sizing means watching utilization at peak and trough and adjusting rather than provisioning once for the worst case.
